title: Windows Logon Process System Error
shortDescription: The Windows Logon Process (winlogon.exe) or Client Server Runtime Subsystem (csrss.exe) terminated unexpectedly.
fullDescription: Status 0xC000021A is a critical system error that occurs when either winlogon.exe or csrss.exe fails. These are essential Windows subsystem processes that Windows cannot run without. When either terminates, Windows forces a system halt to prevent potential data corruption. This is typically caused by corrupted system files, incompatible software, or failed updates.

causes.cause: Corrupted winlogon.exe or csrss.exe system files
Failed Windows Update corrupting system files

symptoms: System halts with STATUS_SYSTEM_PROCESS_TERMINATED
Windows cannot boot past the loading screen
Blue screen immediately after Windows logo
Boot loop after Windows Update

fixSteps.title: Boot into Recovery Environment
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media > Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC offline
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Command Prompt: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

fixSteps.title: Uninstall recent update
fixSteps.instruction: From Advanced Options > Uninstall Updates > Uninstall latest quality update or feature update.

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: From Advanced Options > System Restore. Choose a restore point from before the issue started.

whenToCallPro: If all recovery options fail and Windows cannot boot at all, professional repair or reinstallation may be needed.

faq.q: Why is this error so serious?
faq.a: Winlogon.exe and csrss.exe are critical to Windows operation. Without them, Windows cannot provide basic functionality like user logon or win32 subsystem support.

faq.q: Can I fix this without Windows installation media?
faq.a: If the PC can reach Advanced Startup Options (hold Shift while clicking Restart, or interrupt boot 3 times), you can access recovery tools without media.

faq.q: Will I lose my data?
faq.a: The repair steps preserve your data. Even if a clean install is ultimately needed, you can usually access files by booting from USB first.

title: Boot Configuration Data File Missing
shortDescription: The Windows Boot Configuration Data (BCD) file is missing or corrupted.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000034 indicates that the BCD file required for Windows boot is missing or corrupted. The BCD file contains boot configuration entries that tell the Windows Boot Manager how to start Windows. Without it, the system cannot boot.

causes.cause: Boot sector damage from disk error

symptoms: System will not boot at all
Error message about missing BCD file
Recovery environment loads instead of Windows
Boot menu is empty or shows no entries

fixSteps.title: Boot from Windows installation media
fixSteps.instruction: Insert Windows USB/DVD > Boot from it > Repair your computer > Command Prompt.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: In Command Prompt run: bootrec /fixmbr && bootrec /fixboot && bootrec /scanos && bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Manually recreate BCD
fixSteps.instruction: If bootrec fails: bcdedit /createstore C:\Boot\BCD && bcdedit /store C:\Boot\BCD /create {bootmgr} /d "Windows Boot Manager" && bcdedit /store C:\Boot\BCD /create /d "Windows" /application osloader

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: From recovery options, try Startup Repair which automatically rebuilds boot records.

whenToCallPro: If manual BCD recreation fails and Windows cannot be recovered through any boot repair method.

faq.q: What is the BCD?
faq.a: The Boot Configuration Data is a firmware-independent database for boot-time configuration data. It replaced boot.ini from earlier Windows versions.

faq.q: Can disk cloning cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Improperly cloned disks may have incorrect BCD entries. The BCD must reference the correct disk and partition UUIDs.

faq.q: Does this mean data loss?
faq.a: No. The BCD is a small boot configuration file. Your Windows installation and personal data remain intact on the disk.

title: Required Device Not Connected or Accessible
shortDescription: A required device is not connected or cannot be accessed, preventing Windows from booting.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000225 indicates that Windows Boot Manager cannot find or access a required boot device. This usually means the BCD entry points to a disk or partition that is missing, the boot sector is corrupted, or the system partition is damaged.

causes.cause: Changed disk order in BIOS after adding a drive

symptoms: Error 0xC0000225 on boot screen
Message: A required device is not connected
Error appears after adding or removing a hard drive
Boot fails after BIOS update

fixSteps.title: Check BIOS boot order
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS and verify the boot drive is listed first. If drives were reordered, set the correct one as primary.

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: From recovery Command Prompt: bootrec /fixmbr && bootrec /fixboot && bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Repair system partition
fixSteps.instruction: From Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r to repair the system partition.

whenToCallPro: If the boot drive is not detected in BIOS, it may have failed and require data recovery.

faq.q: Why did this happen after adding a new drive?
faq.a: Adding a drive can change the disk numbering in BIOS, causing the BCD to reference the wrong disk. Correcting the boot order or rebuilding BCD fixes this.

faq.q: Can a BIOS update cause this?
faq.a: Yes. BIOS updates can reset boot order settings and change how disks are enumerated. Check boot order after any BIOS update.

faq.q: Is my data safe?
faq.a: Usually yes. This is a boot configuration issue, not a data loss issue. Your files remain on the drive.

title: Unsuccessful System Operation
shortDescription: An unsuccessful operation prevented the system from starting correctly.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000001 (STATUS_UNSUCCESSFUL) is a generic boot error indicating that an essential operation failed during Windows startup. It can be caused by corrupted system files, damaged SAM registry hive, failing hardware, or corrupted boot configuration.
symptoms: Error 0xC0000001 during boot
Windows cannot start normally
Boot loop after system crash
Recovery Environment loads automatically

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC offline
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Command Prompt: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

fixSteps.title: Restore SAM registry hive
fixSteps.instruction: From Command Prompt: copy C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\SAM C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM (if RegBack has recent copies)

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: From Advanced Options > System Restore.

whenToCallPro: If registry restoration fails and System Restore does not work.

faq.q: What is the SAM hive?
faq.a: The Security Accounts Manager (SAM) registry hive stores user account and security information. If corrupted, Windows cannot authenticate users and fails to boot.

faq.q: Will restoring SAM lose my password?
faq.a: If you restore from a backup, your password may revert to what it was when the backup was made.

faq.q: Can a power failure cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Power loss during a write to the registry can corrupt the SAM or SYSTEM hives, causing this boot failure.

title: Boot Selection Failed - Required Device Inaccessible
shortDescription: The boot selection failed because a required device is inaccessible.
fullDescription: Error 0xC000000E indicates that the Windows Boot Manager could not find the OS partition specified in the BCD. This commonly occurs when the boot drive's disk order changes, the BCD is corrupted, or the system partition is damaged.

causes.cause: BCD pointing to wrong disk or partition
Boot drive not detected or failed

symptoms: Boot error 0xC000000E with device inaccessible message
Windows fails to start after hardware change
Error after cloning or moving disk to new computer
Boot fails after disconnecting secondary drive

fixSteps.title: Verify BIOS boot settings
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS. Confirm the correct drive is set as first boot device and is detected.

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Repair > Troubleshoot > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: From recovery Command Prompt: bootrec /rebuildbcd. If it finds a Windows installation, type Y to add it.

fixSteps.title: Fix BCD device reference
fixSteps.instruction: Use bcdedit to update the device and osdevice entries to point to the correct partition.

whenToCallPro: If the drive is not detected in BIOS or if bcdedit commands cannot resolve the reference.

faq.q: Why did this happen after removing a drive?
faq.a: If the BCD was stored on the removed drive, or if removing it changed the boot drive's disk number, the BCD references become invalid.

faq.q: Can disk cloning cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Cloned disks may have BCD entries pointing to the source disk's UUID. Rebuilding BCD on the cloned disk fixes this.

faq.q: Is the fix the same for UEFI and Legacy BIOS?
faq.a: The general approach is the same, but UEFI systems use different partition structures. Ensure you are working with the correct EFI System Partition.

title: Boot Configuration Data Error
shortDescription: The boot configuration data for the PC is missing or contains errors.
fullDescription: Error 0xC000000F indicates that the BCD file exists but contains errors that prevent Windows from booting. Unlike 0xC0000034 where the BCD is missing entirely, this error means the BCD is present but has invalid or corrupted entries.
symptoms: Boot error message mentioning BCD data
Windows Boot Manager shows error 0xC000000F
Error after modifying boot configuration
Cannot select any boot option

fixSteps.title: Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Repair > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD from scratch
fixSteps.instruction: From Command Prompt: bcdedit /export C:\BCD_Backup && attrib C:\Boot\BCD -h -r -s && del C:\Boot\BCD && bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Fix boot records
fixSteps.instruction: bootrec /fixmbr && bootrec /fixboot

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK on system partition
fixSteps.instruction: chkdsk C: /f to repair any filesystem errors affecting the BCD.

whenToCallPro: If BCD recreation fails repeatedly, there may be underlying disk issues.

faq.q: Can I back up the BCD before fixing it?
faq.a: Yes. Use bcdedit /export to create a backup. This allows you to restore the original BCD if the fix does not work.

faq.q: Is this related to the bootloader?
faq.a: Yes. The BCD is used by the Windows Boot Manager (bootloader) to determine how to start Windows. Corruption prevents the bootloader from working.

faq.q: Can third-party boot managers cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Tools like EasyBCD or GRUB can inadvertently corrupt the Windows BCD if not used carefully.

title: Registry Hive Could Not Be Loaded
shortDescription: One or more registry hives could not be loaded during boot, preventing Windows from starting.
fullDescription: Error 0xC000014C indicates that the Windows kernel could not load one or more critical registry hives (SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, DEFAULT) during boot. These hives are essential for Windows operation. Corruption is usually caused by power loss, disk errors, or malware.

causes.cause: Corrupted SYSTEM or SOFTWARE registry hive
Power loss during registry write operation
Disk errors in registry file area

symptoms: Boot error about registry hive loading failure
Windows cannot start at all
Error appears after unexpected power loss
System enters boot loop

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Advanced Options > System Restore.

fixSteps.title: Restore registry from backup
fixSteps.instruction: From Command Prompt: copy C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\* C:\Windows\System32\config\ (overwrite when prompted). Note: RegBack may be empty on newer Windows 10 versions.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK
fixSteps.instruction: chkdsk C: /f /r to repair disk errors that may have corrupted the registry files.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC offline
fixSteps.instruction: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

whenToCallPro: If registry backup files are not available and System Restore has no restore points.

faq.q: What are registry hives?
faq.a: Registry hives are files in C:\Windows\System32\config that store the Windows registry. They include SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, and DEFAULT.

faq.q: Why is RegBack empty on Windows 10?
faq.a: Microsoft disabled automatic registry backups in Windows 10 version 1803 and later to save disk space. You can re-enable it through a registry setting.

faq.q: Can a UPS prevent this?
faq.a: Yes. An uninterruptible power supply prevents sudden power loss, which is a major cause of registry corruption during write operations.

title: Boot Device Access Failure
shortDescription: The system could not access the boot device properly, indicating I/O or connection issues.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000185 (STATUS_IO_DEVICE_ERROR) indicates an I/O error when accessing the boot device. This commonly points to physical connection issues (loose cables), failing storage hardware, or corrupted disk sectors in the boot area.

causes.cause: Bad sectors in system file area

symptoms: Boot error 0xC0000185 with I/O device message
System cannot find or read boot device
Intermittent boot failures
Drive makes unusual noises

fixSteps.title: Check physical connections
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case. Reseat SATA data and power cables. For NVMe, reseat the M.2 SSD. Try different SATA port.

fixSteps.title: Check drive in BIOS
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS and verify the boot drive is detected with correct capacity.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK from recovery
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r

fixSteps.title: Rebuild boot records
fixSteps.instruction: From Command Prompt: bootrec /fixmbr && bootrec /fixboot && bootrec /rebuildbcd

whenToCallPro: If the drive is not detected in BIOS or shows incorrect capacity, it is likely failing and needs professional data recovery.

faq.q: Is this always a hardware problem?
faq.a: Not always. While hardware issues are the most common cause, corrupted boot sectors can also cause I/O errors. CHKDSK can fix software-level issues.

faq.q: Can a loose cable really cause this?
faq.a: Absolutely. A slightly loose SATA cable can cause intermittent I/O errors. This is one of the most common and cheapest fixes.

faq.q: Should I replace my drive?
faq.a: If the drive is not detected in BIOS or CHKDSK finds many bad sectors, replacement is recommended. Check S.M.A.R.T. data if possible.

title: Bad Image - System File Corrupted
shortDescription: A critical system file is corrupted and cannot be loaded, preventing Windows from starting.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000221 (STATUS_IMAGE_CHECKSUM_MISMATCH) indicates that a critical system DLL or executable has a checksum mismatch, meaning the file is corrupted. Windows cannot load the damaged file and halts boot. The error usually names the specific corrupted file.

causes.cause: System file corrupted by disk error
RAM errors causing file corruption during write
Malware replacing or corrupting system files

symptoms: Boot error naming a specific corrupted file (e.g., ntdll.dll, kernel32.dll)
Bad Image error message during startup
Windows fails to load after update
Random Bad Image errors for different files

fixSteps.title: Run SFC offline
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Command Prompt: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

fixSteps.title: Run DISM offline
fixSteps.instruction: DISM /Image:C:\ /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth /Source:D:\Sources\install.wim (D: = Windows media)

fixSteps.title: Replace specific file manually
fixSteps.instruction: If the error names a specific file, copy it from the Windows installation media: expand D:\Sources\install.wim /F:filename.dll C:\Windows\System32\

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: After fixing boot, run MemTest86 to check if RAM errors are causing file corruption.

whenToCallPro: If multiple system files are corrupted and SFC/DISM cannot repair them.

faq.q: What does checksum mismatch mean?
faq.a: Every system file has a checksum (hash) that verifies its integrity. A mismatch means the file contents have changed from the expected values, indicating corruption.

faq.q: Can I copy the file from another computer?
faq.a: Only if the other computer runs the exact same Windows version and build. Using Windows installation media is safer and more reliable.

faq.q: Why does this keep happening to different files?
faq.a: If multiple files become corrupted over time, the underlying cause is likely failing RAM or a failing drive. Test both to find the root cause.

title: Application Unable to Start - Bad Image Format
shortDescription: An application failed to start because a required DLL has an incorrect format (32/64-bit mismatch).
fullDescription: Error 0xC000007B (STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_FORMAT) occurs when an application tries to load a DLL with an incompatible architecture (e.g., a 64-bit app loading a 32-bit DLL, or vice versa). This commonly affects games and applications that depend on Visual C++ Redistributables, DirectX, or .NET Framework.

causes.cause: Missing or corrupted Visual C++ Redistributable
Missing or corrupted DirectX runtime files

symptoms: Application was unable to start correctly (0xC000007B)
Error appears when launching games or applications
Program crashes immediately on startup
Error appears after Windows Update or runtime update

fixSteps.title: Install all Visual C++ Redistributables
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install all versions (2008, 2010, 2012, 2013, 2015-2022) of Visual C++ Redistributable in both x86 and x64 versions from Microsoft.

fixSteps.title: Install DirectX End-User Runtime
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the DirectX End-User Runtime Web Installer from Microsoft to install all legacy DirectX components.

fixSteps.title: Repair .NET Framework
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the Microsoft .NET Framework Repair Tool.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall the affected application completely, then reinstall it. Use the application's official installer.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists for all applications, there may be deeper system file corruption requiring professional repair.

faq.q: Why do I need both x86 and x64 runtimes?
faq.a: Many 64-bit applications include 32-bit (x86) components or plugins. Both versions of the runtime libraries may be needed even on a 64-bit system.

faq.q: Which Visual C++ versions do I need?
faq.a: Install all versions from 2008 through 2022. Different applications depend on different versions, and they can all coexist without conflict.

faq.q: Can this be caused by a virus?
faq.a: Rarely, but malware that replaces system DLLs can cause this. If the error affects many applications, run a full malware scan.

title: Access Violation - Application Crash
shortDescription: An application attempted to access memory it was not allowed to, causing a crash.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000005 (STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION) is one of the most common Windows application errors. It occurs when a program tries to read or write to a memory address that it does not have permission to access. This can be caused by software bugs, corrupted files, malware, or hardware issues like bad RAM.

causes.cause: Software bug causing invalid memory access

symptoms: Application crashes with access violation error
Error dialog showing 0xC0000005
Application Error in Event Viewer with exception code 0xC0000005
Game crashes during loading or gameplay

fixSteps.title: Update the application
fixSteps.instruction: Check for and install the latest version or patches for the crashing application.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall the application, restart, then reinstall from scratch.

fixSteps.title: Run as administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the application > Properties > Compatibility > Run as administrator. Also try Windows 7/8 compatibility mode.

fixSteps.title: Add DEP exception
fixSteps.instruction: Open System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > DEP. Select Turn on DEP for all programs except those I select and add the application.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: If multiple applications crash, run MemTest86 to check for RAM errors.

whenToCallPro: If multiple applications crash with this error, it may indicate a RAM or system-level problem requiring professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Is an access violation always the application's fault?
faq.a: Usually yes. Most access violations are caused by software bugs. However, faulty RAM can also cause random access violations across multiple programs.

faq.q: Can antivirus cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Some antivirus software injects code into applications for monitoring, which can conflict and cause access violations. Try temporarily disabling it.

faq.q: Should I worry about data loss?
faq.a: The application crash may lose unsaved work, but it does not damage your system or other files. Save frequently when using crash-prone applications.

title: Microsoft Visual C++ Runtime Error
shortDescription: A Visual C++ runtime library error occurred, causing an application to terminate abnormally.
fullDescription: Visual C++ Runtime Errors occur when an application built with Microsoft Visual C++ encounters an error in the runtime library. Common variants include R6025 (pure virtual function call), R6034 (manifest error), and R6002 (floating point not loaded). These are typically caused by corrupted runtime files, software conflicts, or application bugs.
symptoms: Dialog box showing Microsoft Visual C++ Runtime Library Error
Application terminates immediately after launch
Runtime error R6025, R6034, or R6002
Error during specific application operations

fixSteps.title: Repair Visual C++ Redistributables
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Apps. Find all Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable entries. Click Modify > Repair for each one.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall all Visual C++ versions
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall all Visual C++ Redistributable packages, restart, then reinstall all versions (2008 through 2022, both x86 and x64).

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the affected application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall and reinstall the application that triggers the error.

fixSteps.title: Perform clean boot test
fixSteps.instruction: Use msconfig to perform a clean boot and identify if a background service conflicts with the application.

whenToCallPro: If the runtime error affects multiple applications and reinstalling runtimes does not help.

faq.q: What is a Visual C++ Runtime?
faq.a: It is a set of libraries needed by applications built with Microsoft Visual C++. Most Windows applications depend on one or more versions.

faq.q: Why are there so many versions of Visual C++?
faq.a: Each version of Visual Studio produces applications that need its specific runtime. Applications cannot share runtimes across versions, so all required versions must be installed.

faq.q: Can I just install the latest version?
faq.a: No. You need the specific version that each application was compiled with. Install all versions from 2008 through 2022 to cover all applications.

title: .NET Framework Initialization or Runtime Error
shortDescription: The .NET Framework encountered an error during initialization or application execution.
fullDescription: .NET Framework errors occur when the Common Language Runtime (CLR) encounters a problem loading, initializing, or executing a .NET application. Common errors include CLR20r3, System.BadImageFormatException, System.IO.FileNotFoundException, and general framework initialization failures.

causes.cause: Missing .NET Framework version required by application
Windows Update failed to update .NET components

symptoms: Application fails with .NET Framework error
CLR20r3 error in Event Viewer
Application requires a .NET version not installed
Framework initialization errors on startup

fixSteps.title: Run .NET Framework Repair Tool
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the Microsoft .NET Framework Repair Tool from Microsoft's website.

fixSteps.title: Enable .NET Framework 3.5
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Features (optionalfeatures.exe) and ensure .NET Framework 3.5 is enabled. May need Windows media as a source.

fixSteps.title: Install latest .NET runtimes
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest .NET Framework 4.8.x and .NET 6/7/8 runtimes from dotnet.microsoft.com.

fixSteps.title: Run DISM to repair .NET
fixSteps.instruction: DISM /Online /Enable-Feature /FeatureName:NetFx3 /All /Source:D:\Sources\sxs

whenToCallPro: If .NET Framework repair and reinstallation do not resolve the issue, deeper system repair may be needed.

faq.q: What is the difference between .NET Framework and .NET?
faq.a: .NET Framework (up to 4.8) is the legacy version bundled with Windows. .NET (5, 6, 7, 8) is the modern cross-platform version. Some apps need one, some need the other.

faq.q: Why do some apps need .NET 3.5?
faq.a: Older applications were built against .NET 2.0/3.0/3.5. These are included in the .NET 3.5 feature that must be enabled separately in Windows 10/11.

faq.q: Can I have multiple .NET versions installed?
faq.a: Yes. Different .NET Framework versions coexist peacefully. An application uses the specific version it was built for.

title: Application Error - Event ID 1000
shortDescription: An application faulted and was terminated, logged as Event ID 1000 in the Application event log.
fullDescription: Event ID 1000 is logged by Windows Error Reporting (WER) when an application crashes. The event details include the faulting application name, faulting module, exception code, and fault offset. This information is essential for diagnosing the root cause of application crashes.
symptoms: Application crashes without error dialog
Event ID 1000 entries in Application log
Faulting module identified in event details
Repeated crashes of the same application

fixSteps.title: Check Event Viewer details
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Application. Find Event ID 1000 entries. Note the Faulting application name and Faulting module name.

fixSteps.title: Update the application
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest version or patches for the faulting application.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the faulting module
fixSteps.instruction: If the faulting module is a system DLL (like ntdll.dll), run sfc /scannow. If it is an app DLL, reinstall the application.

fixSteps.title: Update Visual C++ and .NET runtimes
fixSteps.instruction: Install all Visual C++ Redistributable versions and update .NET Framework.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting module is a system DLL and SFC cannot repair it.

faq.q: How do I read Event ID 1000 details?
faq.a: Look for Faulting application name (the crashing program), Faulting module name (the specific DLL causing the crash), and Exception code (the type of error).

faq.q: What does ntdll.dll as faulting module mean?
faq.a: ntdll.dll is a core Windows library. When it appears as faulting module, the actual cause is usually the calling application, not ntdll.dll itself.

faq.q: Should I report the crash to the developer?
faq.a: If the application is maintained, yes. Include the Event ID 1000 details, especially the exception code and fault offset.

title: DCOM Permissions Error
shortDescription: A DCOM server could not be started because the configured permissions do not grant the required access.
fullDescription: Event ID 10016 from DistributedCOM is one of the most common entries in the Windows System event log. It occurs when a DCOM (Distributed Component Object Model) component attempts to start but the requesting user or service does not have the required Launch or Activation permissions in the DCOM configuration.

causes.cause: Default DCOM permissions not matching component requirements
Windows Update changing DCOM component permissions
Third-party software installing DCOM components with wrong permissions

symptoms: Event ID 10016 flooding the System event log
Multiple DCOM permission errors for different CLSIDs
Typically does not cause visible problems
May cause some Windows Store apps to not function properly

fixSteps.title: Identify the DCOM component
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer, find Event 10016. Note the CLSID and APPID values. Search these in Component Services (dcomcnfg).

fixSteps.title: Take ownership of registry key
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor. Navigate to HKCR\CLSID\{CLSID-value}. Right-click > Permissions > Advanced > Change owner to Administrators.

fixSteps.title: Edit DCOM permissions
fixSteps.instruction: Open Component Services (dcomcnfg) > Computers > My Computer > DCOM Config. Find the component, right-click > Properties > Security > Edit launch and activation permissions.

fixSteps.title: Consider ignoring benign entries
fixSteps.instruction: Many DCOM 10016 errors are benign and do not affect system operation. Microsoft has acknowledged some as expected behavior.

whenToCallPro: If DCOM errors are causing actual functionality problems (Store apps not working, services failing to start).

faq.q: Are DCOM 10016 errors serious?
faq.a: Usually not. Most DCOM 10016 errors are benign and do not affect system operation. Microsoft considers some of them expected behavior.

faq.q: Should I fix every 10016 error?
faq.a: Only fix them if they are causing actual problems. The effort to fix each one is significant, and most have no impact on system functionality.

faq.q: Why are there so many of these?
faq.a: Windows has hundreds of DCOM components, and permission mismatches are common, especially after updates. Microsoft has not prioritized fixing them because they are mostly harmless.

title: System File Checker Found Integrity Violations
shortDescription: System File Checker (SFC) detected corrupted system files that it could not repair.
fullDescription: When SFC (sfc /scannow) reports that it found corrupt files but was unable to fix some of them, it means the Windows Resource Protection mechanism detected system files that do not match their expected versions, but the repair source was also corrupted or unavailable. The CBS.log file contains details about which files are affected.

causes.cause: Failed Windows Update leaving partial files

symptoms: SFC reports: found corrupt files but unable to fix some
Multiple SFC runs produce same result
CBS.log shows specific corrupted files
Windows features or applications malfunction

fixSteps.title: Run DISM RestoreHealth first
fixSteps.instruction: DISM must repair the component store before SFC can work. Run: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Run SFC again
fixSteps.instruction: After DISM completes: sfc /scannow. SFC should now be able to repair files using the restored component store.

fixSteps.title: Use Windows media as DISM source
fixSteps.instruction: If DISM fails: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth /Source:D:\Sources\install.wim /LimitAccess

fixSteps.title: Check CBS.log for details
fixSteps.instruction: Open C:\Windows\Logs\CBS\CBS.log. Search for Cannot repair to see exactly which files are corrupted.

whenToCallPro: If DISM with an installation media source also fails to repair the component store.

faq.q: Why does SFC fail to repair?
faq.a: SFC uses the component store (WinSxS) as its repair source. If the component store itself is corrupted, SFC has no good copy to restore from. DISM must fix the store first.

faq.q: Should I run SFC or DISM first?
faq.a: Always run DISM /RestoreHealth first, then SFC. DISM repairs the component store, which SFC then uses to repair system files.

faq.q: Is it safe to ignore SFC errors?
faq.a: Minor SFC errors may not cause noticeable problems. However, they should be addressed to prevent accumulating corruption.

title: Unable to Locate DLL
shortDescription: The application could not start because a required DLL was not found on the system.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000135 (STATUS_DLL_NOT_FOUND) occurs when an application references a DLL that is not present on the system. The application cannot load without this dependency and fails to start.

causes.cause: DLL deleted by cleanup tool or antivirus

symptoms: Application fails to start with DLL not found error
Error dialog naming the missing DLL file
Program worked before but stopped after cleanup or update
Multiple applications fail to start

fixSteps.title: Identify and install the missing runtime
fixSteps.instruction: Note the DLL name in the error. Search online to determine which runtime package provides it (Visual C++, DirectX, .NET, etc.) and install it.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall and reinstall the application. The installer should install required dependencies.

fixSteps.title: Install all common runtimes
fixSteps.instruction: Install Visual C++ Redistributables (all versions, x86 and x64), DirectX End-User Runtime, and .NET Framework.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: If the missing DLL is a Windows system file, run: sfc /scannow

whenToCallPro: If the missing DLL is a system file that SFC cannot repair.

faq.q: Should I download DLLs from the internet?
faq.a: Never download individual DLL files from third-party websites. They may contain malware. Always install the complete runtime package or reinstall the application.

faq.q: Why did the DLL disappear?
faq.a: Common causes include antivirus false positives quarantining the file, cleanup tools removing it, or an incomplete application update.

faq.q: Can I copy the DLL from another computer?
faq.a: This is not recommended. The DLL version must match exactly. Installing the proper runtime package ensures the correct version.

title: Application Failed to Initialize Properly
shortDescription: The application was unable to start correctly due to a DLL initialization failure.
fullDescription: Error 0xC0000142 (STATUS_DLL_INIT_FAILED) occurs when a DLL that the application depends on fails during its initialization routine (DllMain). This can be caused by corrupted DLLs, conflicting software, or resource exhaustion.
symptoms: Application was unable to start correctly (0xC0000142)
Error appears for cmd.exe or other system tools
Multiple applications fail to start
Error appears after Windows Update

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Perform clean boot
fixSteps.instruction: msconfig > Services > Hide all Microsoft services > Disable all. Restart and test.

fixSteps.title: Create new user profile
fixSteps.instruction: Create a new local admin account and test the application from the new profile.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall Visual C++ runtimes
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall and reinstall all Visual C++ Redistributable packages.

whenToCallPro: If system tools like cmd.exe are affected, the system corruption may be severe enough to require professional repair.

faq.q: What does DLL initialization failure mean?
faq.a: When a DLL is loaded, its DllMain function runs initialization code. If this code fails (due to corruption, missing dependencies, or resource issues), the DLL cannot be used.

faq.q: Why does cmd.exe get this error?
faq.a: If core Windows DLLs are corrupted, even basic tools like cmd.exe can fail to initialize. This indicates serious system file corruption.

faq.q: Does a new user profile help?
faq.a: If the error is caused by user-specific settings or corrupted profile data, a new profile eliminates those factors.

title: Unexpected Shutdown - Kernel Power Event 41
shortDescription: The system rebooted without cleanly shutting down first, indicating a crash, power loss, or hardware issue.
fullDescription: Event ID 41 from Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power is logged when Windows starts after an unexpected shutdown. It indicates the previous shutdown was not clean - caused by a crash, power failure, held power button, or hardware reset. It is one of the most common and important diagnostic events.

causes.cause: System crash (BSOD) that did not generate a dump
Power outage or loose power cable

symptoms: Computer restarts unexpectedly
Event ID 41 Kernel-Power in System log
No BSOD screen visible before restart
System shuts off suddenly during use

fixSteps.title: Check Event Viewer for preceding events
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer > System log. Look at events immediately before the Event 41 for clues about the cause.

fixSteps.title: Monitor temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWMonitor and check CPU and GPU temperatures. Overheating causes emergency shutdowns.

fixSteps.title: Check power supply
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all power connections. For desktops, test with a different PSU or use a PSU tester. For laptops, try with and without battery.

fixSteps.title: Disable Fast Startup
fixSteps.instruction: Control Panel > Power Options > Choose what the power buttons do > Change settings > Uncheck Turn on fast startup.

whenToCallPro: If unexpected shutdowns continue after checking temperatures and power supply, professional hardware diagnosis is recommended.

faq.q: Is Event 41 always a problem?
faq.a: Not always. It also logs after manual power-offs (holding the power button), power outages, or forced restarts. Investigate only if it occurs unexpectedly.

faq.q: Why is there no BSOD?
faq.a: Some hardware failures (PSU, overheating) cut power instantly before Windows can display a BSOD or save a crash dump.

faq.q: Can a failing PSU cause this?
faq.a: Yes. An unstable or failing power supply is one of the most common causes of sudden shutdowns without BSOD.

title: WMI Event Filter Error
shortDescription: A WMI event filter query failed due to an invalid namespace, class, or query syntax.
fullDescription: Event ID 10 from WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) indicates that a WMI event subscription has an invalid query. This is commonly caused by orphaned WMI subscriptions from uninstalled software, or built-in Windows subscriptions that reference components not present on the system.

causes.cause: Orphaned WMI subscription from uninstalled software
Built-in Windows subscription referencing absent component

symptoms: Event ID 10 entries in Application log
WMI errors referencing specific namespaces or classes
Usually no visible impact on system operation
Errors may fill event log over time

fixSteps.title: Identify the subscription
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer and note the full error details including the namespace and query. This identifies which component is responsible.

fixSteps.title: Remove orphaned subscription
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated PowerShell. Use Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter to list subscriptions. Remove orphaned ones.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild WMI repository
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: winmgmt /salvagerepository. If that fails: winmgmt /resetrepository

fixSteps.title: Consider ignoring benign errors
fixSteps.instruction: Many WMI Event 10 errors are harmless. If they do not affect system functionality, they can be safely ignored.

whenToCallPro: If WMI errors are causing actual functionality issues (management tools failing, scripts not working).

faq.q: Are WMI Event 10 errors serious?
faq.a: Usually not. Most are harmless orphaned subscriptions that generate log entries but do not affect system operation.

faq.q: What is WMI?
faq.a: Windows Management Instrumentation is an infrastructure for managing data and operations on Windows systems. It is used by scripts, management tools, and Windows itself.

faq.q: Will resetting the WMI repository cause problems?
faq.a: It can affect applications that registered WMI providers. Use /salvagerepository first, which is less destructive. Use /resetrepository only as a last resort.

title: CHKDSK Disk Errors Detected
shortDescription: CHKDSK detected filesystem errors, bad sectors, or index inconsistencies on the disk.
fullDescription: When CHKDSK reports errors, it indicates problems with the filesystem structure on the disk. These can range from minor index inconsistencies to serious bad sectors. The severity depends on whether errors are in the filesystem metadata (fixable) or indicate physical disk damage (requires replacement).

causes.cause: Aging hard drive developing bad sectors

symptoms: CHKDSK reports errors during scan
Windows suggests scanning the drive for errors
Files become corrupted or inaccessible
Drive performance degrades

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK with repair
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r. Schedule the scan for next restart if the drive is in use.

fixSteps.title: Check S.M.A.R.T. status
fixSteps.instruction: Download CrystalDiskInfo. Check Reallocated Sectors Count and Current Pending Sector Count.

fixSteps.title: Back up data immediately
fixSteps.instruction: If bad sectors are found, back up all important data to another drive immediately before the drive potentially fails further.

fixSteps.title: Replace drive if necessary
fixSteps.instruction: If S.M.A.R.T. shows degradation or CHKDSK finds many bad sectors, replace the drive.

whenToCallPro: If the drive has critical data and is failing, professional data recovery may be needed.

faq.q: Can CHKDSK fix all disk errors?
faq.a: CHKDSK can fix filesystem metadata errors and mark bad sectors so they are not used. It cannot repair physically damaged sectors or recover data from them.

faq.q: How long does CHKDSK take?
faq.a: A basic scan takes minutes. A full scan with /r flag (surface scan) can take 1-6 hours depending on drive size.

faq.q: Should I run CHKDSK on an SSD?
faq.a: CHKDSK /f (fix filesystem) is fine on SSDs. Avoid /r (surface scan) on SSDs as it is unnecessary - SSDs handle bad blocks internally via their controller.

title: Windows Activation Error - No Valid License
shortDescription: Windows could not be activated because no valid digital license or product key was found.
fullDescription: Error 0x803F7001 indicates that Windows cannot find a valid activation license or product key for the current hardware configuration. This commonly occurs after significant hardware changes (motherboard replacement), after a clean install without entering a product key, or when the digital license is tied to a different hardware configuration.

causes.cause: Clean install without product key entry
Digital license linked to different Microsoft account
OEM license used on non-original hardware

symptoms: Activate Windows watermark on desktop
Settings shows Windows is not activated
Cannot personalize desktop or settings
Error 0x803F7001 in activation settings

fixSteps.title: Run activation troubleshooter
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Update & Security > Activation > Troubleshoot. If you changed hardware, select I changed hardware on this device recently.

fixSteps.title: Link Microsoft account
fixSteps.instruction: Sign in with the Microsoft account that was linked to the digital license before the hardware change. Go to Activation > Add an account.

fixSteps.title: Enter product key
fixSteps.instruction: If you have a product key: Settings > Activation > Change product key. Enter your 25-character product key.

fixSteps.title: Contact Microsoft support
fixSteps.instruction: If the troubleshooter cannot activate, use the Get Help app or call Microsoft support for manual activation.

whenToCallPro: If you need a new Windows license due to OEM restrictions or cannot recover your digital license.

faq.q: Can I reactivate after changing my motherboard?
faq.a: If your license is a retail license linked to a Microsoft account, yes. Use the activation troubleshooter and select I changed hardware. OEM licenses are tied to the original motherboard.

faq.q: What is a digital license?
faq.a: A digital license (digital entitlement) is an activation method in Windows 10/11 that ties your license to your hardware configuration and Microsoft account, without needing a product key.

faq.q: Is my license OEM or Retail?
faq.a: Open elevated Command Prompt: slmgr /dli. The Description field shows whether it is OEM, Retail, or Volume licensed.

title: Persistent 100% Disk Usage
shortDescription: The system drive shows 100% disk utilization in Task Manager, causing severe performance degradation.
fullDescription: Persistent 100% disk usage is a very common Windows issue where the system drive is constantly at maximum utilization, making the entire system extremely slow. This is especially prevalent on systems with traditional hard drives (HDDs). Common causes include Windows Search indexing, Superfetch/SysMain, Windows Update, and faulty drivers.

causes.cause: Windows Search indexing consuming disk I/O
Windows Update downloading or installing in background
Disk firmware issue or failing drive

symptoms: Task Manager shows Disk column at 100%
System is extremely slow and unresponsive
Applications take minutes to open
Mouse cursor lags or freezes
Boot takes unusually long

fixSteps.title: Disable SysMain service
fixSteps.instruction: Open services.msc. Find SysMain (or Superfetch). Set Startup type to Disabled and Stop the service.

fixSteps.title: Disable Windows Search indexing
fixSteps.instruction: Open services.msc. Find Windows Search. Set to Disabled and Stop. Or selectively exclude drives from indexing.

fixSteps.title: Check for Windows Update activity
fixSteps.instruction: Open Task Manager > Details. Check if svchost.exe or TiWorker.exe is consuming disk. Wait for updates to complete.

fixSteps.title: Update AHCI/SATA driver
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest storage controller driver from your motherboard manufacturer instead of the generic Microsoft driver.

fixSteps.title: Upgrade to SSD
fixSteps.instruction: The most effective permanent fix is upgrading from HDD to SSD. Clone the existing drive to an SSD.

whenToCallPro: If 100% disk usage persists after all software fixes, the drive may be failing. Professional diagnosis can confirm.

faq.q: Is this normal on HDDs?
faq.a: Unfortunately, somewhat. Windows 10/11 performs many background tasks that overwhelm traditional HDDs. An SSD is the best permanent solution.

faq.q: Will disabling SysMain hurt performance?
faq.a: On HDD systems, disabling SysMain usually improves perceived performance because the disk I/O reduction outweighs the caching benefit. On SSD systems, SysMain is typically not problematic.

faq.q: How much does an SSD help?
faq.a: Dramatically. Upgrading from HDD to SSD is the single most impactful performance improvement for most computers. Boot times, application launches, and overall responsiveness improve by 5-10x.

title: Unexpected I/O Error During Boot
shortDescription: An unexpected I/O error occurred during Windows boot, typically indicating a hardware communication problem.
fullDescription: Error 0xC00000E9 (STATUS_UNEXPECTED_IO_ERROR) indicates that Windows encountered an I/O error while communicating with the boot device. This is almost always a hardware connection issue - loose cables, failing ports, or a drive that is intermittently disconnecting.

causes.cause: Loose SATA data or power cable

symptoms: Boot error 0xC00000E9
Message about unexpected I/O error
Error is intermittent - sometimes boots fine
Error appeared after moving or transporting the PC

fixSteps.title: Reseat all drive cables
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, disconnect and reconnect all SATA data and power cables. Ensure they click firmly into place.

fixSteps.title: Try different SATA port
fixSteps.instruction: Move the drive's SATA data cable to a different SATA port on the motherboard.

fixSteps.title: Replace SATA cable
fixSteps.instruction: Try a new SATA data cable. Cables can develop intermittent faults, especially if bent or kinked.

fixSteps.title: Check drive health
fixSteps.instruction: If the PC boots, run CrystalDiskInfo to check S.M.A.R.T. status. Also run chkdsk C: /f /r.

whenToCallPro: If cable replacement does not fix the issue and the drive's S.M.A.R.T. status shows degradation.

faq.q: Why is this intermittent?
faq.a: Loose cables or failing ports can cause intermittent connections. The drive works when the connection is good but fails when it momentarily disconnects.

faq.q: Can laptop movement cause this?
faq.a: On laptops with HDDs, movement can cause momentary read failures. SSDs are not affected by physical movement. Consider upgrading to an SSD.

faq.q: Should I replace the cable or the drive?
faq.a: Try the cable first - it is the cheapest and most common fix. Only replace the drive if SMART data shows degradation.

title: Windows Installer Service Error
shortDescription: The Windows Installer service encountered an error during software installation or update.
fullDescription: Windows Installer (MSI) errors occur when the Windows Installer service fails to install, update, or remove software. Common error codes include 1603 (fatal error), 1722 (script error), 2502/2503 (permission error), and 1719 (service not found). These affect both third-party software and Windows components.

causes.cause: Windows Installer service not running or corrupted

symptoms: Software installation fails with MSI error
Cannot install, update, or remove programs
Installation rolls back during process

fixSteps.title: Re-register Windows Installer
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: msiexec /unregister then msiexec /regserver

fixSteps.title: Start Windows Installer service
fixSteps.instruction: Open services.msc, find Windows Installer, set to Manual, click Start.

fixSteps.title: Run as administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the installer file and select Run as administrator.

fixSteps.title: Use Microsoft Program Install/Uninstall Troubleshooter
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the Microsoft Program Install and Uninstall troubleshooter from Microsoft's website.

whenToCallPro: If the Windows Installer service is severely corrupted and cannot be re-registered.

faq.q: What is Windows Installer error 1603?
faq.a: Error 1603 is a generic fatal error during installation. It usually means a previous installation of the same product exists, or there are permission issues.

faq.q: What are errors 2502 and 2503?
faq.a: These indicate the installer does not have proper permissions. Running the installer as administrator or fixing the Temp folder permissions usually resolves them.

faq.q: Can I manually delete MSI cache?
faq.a: Never delete the C:\Windows\Installer folder. It contains data needed to repair and uninstall programs. Deleting it will break installed software.

title: Group Policy Processing Error
shortDescription: Windows failed to process Group Policy settings, which may affect system configuration and security.
fullDescription: Event ID 1096 from GroupPolicy indicates that Windows failed to read or apply Group Policy objects. This affects system configuration, security settings, and managed deployments. It is common in domain-joined environments but can also affect standalone systems with local Group Policy modifications.

causes.cause: Network connectivity issue to domain controller

symptoms: Event ID 1096 in Application log
Group Policy settings not applying correctly
Login scripts not running
Drive mappings not appearing

fixSteps.title: Force Group Policy update
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: gpupdate /force

fixSteps.title: Clear local GP cache
fixSteps.instruction: Delete files in C:\Windows\System32\GroupPolicy\Machine\Registry.pol and User\Registry.pol. Run gpupdate /force.

fixSteps.title: Verify network connectivity
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the computer can reach the domain controller: ping dc-hostname and nslookup domain.name

fixSteps.title: Run GP Results diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run: gpresult /h C:\GPReport.html and open the HTML file to review applied and failed policies.

whenToCallPro: In enterprise environments, Group Policy issues often require domain admin intervention.

faq.q: Does this affect home/standalone computers?
faq.a: Rarely. Group Policy is mainly used in business/enterprise environments. Standalone PCs may see errors if local policy was manually configured.

faq.q: Can I ignore this error?
faq.a: In enterprise environments, no. GP failures mean security policies and configurations are not being applied correctly. In home environments, it is usually not impactful.

faq.q: What is gpupdate /force?
faq.a: It forces an immediate refresh of all Group Policy settings instead of waiting for the next scheduled refresh (typically every 90 minutes).
